column1: Visualising Roman Quarrying Landscapes in the Inland Balkans
With the Roman arrival in the inland Balkans
column2: stone became essential for construction and an integral material in various aspects of Roman culture. Its durability made it a preferred
column3: long-lasting material across the Roman Empire
column4: and the provinces of the inland Balkans were no exception. The region’s complex geological landscape enabled the exploitation of multiple stone types with varying properties and uses. In recent years
column5: archaeological scholarship has turned its attention to identifying quarrying zones and stone supply in the region to determine which quarries met the stone demand. This study examined local stone resources to understand what was available in the inland Balkans - specifically the parts of Roman provinces of Pannonia
column11: and through that availability clarifies the demand for imported stone.

column9: and architectural elements. Quarrying in the inland Balkans conformed to the broader Roman imperial pattern of resource exploitation
column10: where extraction was concentrated near settlements to minimise transportation costs.

column1: This project focuses on local stone resources in the inland Balkans
column2: drawing primarily on existing archaeological publications to establish the extent and nature of stone exploitation in the region. While several quarry sites were visited in the field
column3: the project did not set out to identify previously unknown Roman quarries; it is acknowledged that more quarries and potential source areas likely exist beyond those presented on this map. The aim was rather to provide as comprehensive a regional overview of extraction activity as possible.

column1: As the inland Balkans also relied on stone imported from neighbouring regions
column2: a selection of the quarries that most frequently supplied stone to the area has been included.

column1: As the exact locations of some Roman quarries or potential stone source areas are not known
column2: the mapped points represent approximate positions. In such cases
column3: the nearest location to the modern settlement associated with the quarry or potential stone source area has been used. In other instances
column4: quarries or potential source areas are represented within an approximate 5 km radius around a central point (such as a district
column6: or ridge) or aligned with the geographical name identified in OpenStreetMap.

column1: This project represents the current state of research and available data at the time of development. It is recognised that the number of Roman quarries and/or potential source areas in the inland Balkans was likely higher
column2: and they could be incorporated to further improve the map. We welcome new information and would be happy to include new data if it is shared with us in the appropriate shapefile format.
